Title: Johor polls will not affect federal government stability, Tok Mat says
Article:
MUAR: The results of the 16th Johor state election will not affect the stability of the federal government despite the country's changing political landscape and dynamics, said Barisan Nasional deputy chairman Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan.
Mohamad said administrative mandates at the federal and state levels have different moulds, and the current political models in several other states prove that this uniqueness can be practised without causing any issues.
"Many states such as Kedah, Perlis, Kelantan, and Terengganu have no problems.
"In Sabah, it is unique—the party is in the opposition but is with the unity government at the centre.
"So the political dynamics of Malaysia have changed; we should not be tied to the old way of thinking," he said.
Mohamad said this to reporters after greeting voters who were having breakfast at a stall in Jalan Sungai Abong here, alongside BN candidate for the Simpang Jeram constituency Datuk Azman Ismail and BN candidate for Bukit Naning Mohd Ghazali Sabari @ Atan.
Commenting on socio-economic aspects, Mohamad said political stability under BN was critical to ensure Johor continued to drive foreign investment inflows, which are currently ahead of other states.
He said the surge in economic performance would translate into increased state revenue, which would ultimately be returned to the people.
"Johor is a state at the forefront of accepting foreign investors. This is a very good thing; in two or three years, it will be translated into state revenue.
"We have seen the state's results from the performance of the Menteri Besar, which he gave to the people of Johor.
"So, people of Johor, do not make a mistake. Continue to support Barisan Nasional to govern Johor, and together we will enjoy the results of our support later," he said.
Touching on Pas' recent statement implying vote support for BN candidates in certain areas, Mohamad reminded the party machinery not to get too comfortable or hope for mercy from outside parties.
"We must continue to work hard. In this life, we should not expect mercy from others. We build our strength to be strong on our own," he said.
The Johor election sees 172 candidates contesting 56 state seats, with polling day set for July 11 and early voting on July 7. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor voters want stability, cost-of-living focus in state election
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor voters want political stability to remain a priority for the government chosen in the 16th Johor election, alongside a strong focus on the cost of living and job opportunities.
Businessman Brian Tan Chong Beng, 67, said a stable government would ensure that existing policies and development projects continue without disruption.
Under the leadership of caretaker menteri besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi, Johor recorded RM110 billion in investments last year, making it the highest in the country's history.
He said the success in attracting high-impact investments to the state would not have been possible without the extensive experience and strong relations between the Johor government and foreign investors.
Tan, who is the government affairs bureau chief of the Johor Chinese Associations Federation, said Onn Hafiz's leadership qualities justified giving him the opportunity to continue administering the state.
"Voters need to give him a strong mandate. His administrative record over the last four years has been successful.
"As such, we should not turn this state election into an experiment, because it is the people who will lose out," he said.
He also said the cost of living should be prioritised to support middle- and low-income groups in the state.
Rising prices of goods, house rentals and daily expenses are placing pressure on the people, especially since the outbreak of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.
"I hope the state government can help ease the financial burden on this group through various appropriate assistance," he said.
[[nid:1477006]]
Meanwhile, first-time voters are excited about the state election and the voting process.
Putera Aniq Zakwan Kama Azam, 18, said he wants youth-related issues such as education and job opportunities to be highlighted by contesting parties and candidates.
"I feel excited and proud to vote for the first time. At the same time, I am a bit nervous knowing that every vote carries value and can determine the future of the state.
"I hope candidates will highlight issues close to youths, such as education and job opportunities for school leavers," he said.
G. Tiwanes, 18, hoped that future state leadership would emphasise harmony among the multi-ethnic communities in the state.
"Strong unity is the foundation of state stability and must continue to be preserved through inclusive policies and approaches.
"I really hope elected leaders will foster harmony among the people, which is now increasingly eroding, through various proactive strategies," he said.
The nomination process for the 16th Johor election concluded last Saturday, with 172 candidates confirmed to contest the 56 state seats, officially kicking off the campaign period ahead of polling day on July 11.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE PM JUNE 30, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:

In the cryptocurrency market, Bitcoin traded lower at RM241,600, while Ethereum also declined to RM6,457.
That's it for Market Pulse.

Title: Police hunt for Monaco bomb suspect after Ukrainian-born businessman wounded
Article:
MONACO: Police on Tuesday were searching for the suspect behind a parcel bomb that wounded a businessman of Ukrainian origin and two others in Monaco, after the unprecedented attack rocked the super-safe principality.
Dozens of officers were deployed in Monaco, while two helicopters and some 30 gendarmes scoured neighbouring France for a man who left a package in a residential building near the border, according to the police and gendarmerie.
The device exploded at around 9pm (1900 GMT) on Monday, leaving a man and a woman seriously wounded and a teenager with lighter injuries, according to the Monegasque authorities.
Monaco public prosecutor Stephane Thibault said as of Tuesday the man had been stabilised, but the woman's condition remained "life-threatening."
He said the blast was being investigated as "attempted murder" but was not being considered as a "terrorist" act.
He declined to say who was the presumed target of the blast, but several sources have said it was Ukrainian-born businessman Vadym Yermolaiev, who is a permanent resident of Monaco and has acquired Cypriot nationality.
Monaco's Minister of State Christophe Mirmand said he was not aware of any specific threats against Yermolaiev.
"The family's behaviour before entering their block of flats did not appear to show any signs of concern on their part," he told broadcaster BFMTV.
"They were dressed for summer, relaxed and did not appear to be taking any particular precautions," he added.
He earlier said the teenager was "very likely related" to the couple.
The wounded had been taken to hospital in the southern French city of Nice. A source close to the case had said the man had suffered severe burns and the woman was in critical condition.
John Bulanadi, a 19-year-old student living near the site of the incident, told AFPTV he had heard a loud explosion.
"I quickly went out onto my terrace to see what was happening. There was screaming, crying and two people on the ground," he said.
Monaco's Prince Albert II described the incident as a "heinous crime" and "a shock to the entire Monegasque community."
- BOLTS, BUCKSHOT -
Thibault said a suspect had left a bag or package in the building's lobby before leaving.
The Monaco government said the "strong explosion" was caused by a "parcel bomb."
"A suspect was seen on video surveillance fleeing towards the municipality of Beausoleil in France," it wrote on X.
Mirmand earlier said witnesses had provided information to identify the suspect.
The explosive device apparently contained bolts and buckshot, he added.
Emergency services treated four other people for shock and cuts from windows shattered in the blast, he said.
"This is the first time in history, to my knowledge, that such an act has taken place in the principality," he added.
Yermolaiev, a multi-millionaire Monaco resident, has been subject to sanctions from Kyiv since December 2023, which Ukrainian security services reportedly said stemmed from his alcohol business activity in Russian-occupied Crimea.
Mirmand told a news conference late Monday that intelligences services were working to understand the victims' background and "determine if others might be facing specific threats."--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: World Cup betting: Trio now detained under Immigration Act after remand extension denied
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Three foreign men remain in custody under the Immigration Act after the magistrate's court here today rejected police's application to extend their remand.
Penang police chief Datuk Dennis Lim said the suspects were initially remanded for four days from June 27 to 30 to facilitate investigations for World Cup football betting activities.
He said all the detainees were brought before the George Town magistrate's court for an application to extend their remand by another three days.
"However, the magistrate did not allow the application.
"Following the court's decision, a Malaysian woman was released on police bail, while the three foreign men were detained under Section 51(5)(b) of the Immigration Act 1959/63 until July 9," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Lim said the investigation paper would be completed and referred to the deputy public prosecutor for further action.
Magistrate Nadratun Naim Mohd Said had
dismissed the application
involving the four suspects, aged between 29 and 44.
They were arrested during a raid on a condominium unit at The Light Collection I in Gelugor last Friday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Migration helps a stagnating world to prosper
Article:
LETTERS:
Migration, in its physical, professional, and intellectual forms, is the engine of human life.
Yet, we find ourselves in an era where the migrant is vilified, even though nearly every cornerstone of modern civilisation is a testament to the power of movement.
Consider the United States — a nation built wave after wave by the Irish, Italians, Chinese, Africans (forced, tragically, but migrants nonetheless), and Latin Americans. It is the ultimate mosaic of migration.
So, why is this same nation now erecting walls and dismantling asylum systems? The answer lies not in economics, but in psychology.
When a society is at peace with its identity, it celebrates the migrant. When it is anxious — economically or culturally — the migrant becomes a convenient scapegoat for wage stagnation and housing crises.
In Malaysia, we have built an economic empire not on native flora, but on two botanical migrants: the oil palm from West Africa and rubber from the Amazon basin.
These plants did not belong to our land. Yet, through research, adaptation, and sheer tenacity, these migrant crops became the backbone of our economy and global food security.
Take palm oil. If Malaysia had not embraced this African migrant, the world would be facing a catastrophic edible oil deficit. As populations grow, demand for cooking oil rises; if supply were restricted to soybean, sunflower, or rapeseed, prices would spiral beyond the reach of the global poor.
India, for instance, is acutely sensitive to oil prices; a shortage there doesn't just mean higher grocery bills — it means social unrest. Palm oil, because of its yield efficiency, keeps the global market stable.
It has lifted millions of smallholder farmers in Southeast Asia and Africa out of destitution, brought prosperity to the producer and affordability to the consumer.
If we can celebrate the migration of plants for the good they bring, why do we fear the migration of people for the same reason?
A 2018 study by the New American Economy found that immigrants in the US are nearly twice as likely to start a new business as native-born citizens. They are the palm oil of human capital — filling gaps, stabilising markets, and driving innovation where locals may have stagnated.
Of course, the critics will argue that unchecked migration strains infrastructure. And they are right to demand order. But there is a vast difference between managing a flow and demonising the fluid.
The issue is the lack of adaptive infrastructure. We have allowed our education systems, housing policies, and social safety nets to become brittle, and instead of fixing them, we blame the migrants for breaking them.
Every nation-state is a historical construct of converging tribes. To be against migration is to be against the very process that created the nation you are defending. It is to deny biology, history, and economic reality.
The world celebrated the "Green Revolution" for saving billions from starvation; we should equally celebrate the "Migration Revolution" for saving civilisations from stagnation.
The oil palm didn't demand cultural assimilation; it thrived and fed the masses. So, too, the rubber tree and many other migrant crops. Human migrants are no different.
It is time we realise that migration is not a threat to be managed, but a gift to be cultivated. After all, we are all migrants. Some of us just haven't moved recently enough to remember it.
DR AHMAD IBRAHIM
Professor,
Tan Sri Omar Centre for STI Policy,
UCSI University
* The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tropicana, VitaHealth revive Offline 16 mental wellness initiative for third year
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tropicana Corp Bhd and Australian-owned supplement brand VitaHealth Malaysia are bringing back their mental wellness initiative, Offline 16, for a third consecutive year as part of efforts to promote healthier digital habits and strengthen community engagement.
The programme forms part of both companies' environmental, social and governance (ESG) commitments, with the initiative aimed at encouraging individuals to reduce screen time and reconnect through offline activities and social interaction.
The latest edition, Offline 16: Dance & Busking Festival, will be held on July 12, 2026, at Tropicana Golf & Country Resort, encouraging participants to take a break from digital devices and reconnect through music, dance and face-to-face social interaction.
Launched as a digital detox campaign, Offline 16 has expanded into a regional mental wellness movement, recording nine editions across Malaysia and Singapore, attracting 1,645 participants and accumulating 46 offline hours. The initiative has also been recognised by the Malaysia Book of Records.
Chief executive officer of Vita Life Sciences Malaysia and Singapore, Dr KS Beh, who founded Offline 16, said the initiative reflects VitaHealth's commitment to promoting holistic wellness beyond nutritional health.
"With over 79 years of heritage as a trusted nutrition brand, we remain committed not only to delivering quality supplements but also to championing meaningful initiatives that inspire healthier, happier lifestyles.
"What began as a simple challenge, encouraging people to put away their phones and reconnect with life, has since grown into a broader mental wellness movement," Beh said.
Tropicana Foundation programme director and Tropicana Corp senior general manager for group corporate communications and ESG, Serena Lim, said the property developer's partnership with VitaHealth has entered its third year after successfully engaging more than 1,600 participants nationwide.
She said the collaboration aligns closely with Tropicana's ESG objectives as a community builder, with the initiative promoting healthier lifestyles, meaningful placemaking and stronger community engagement beyond residential development.
Lim added that the programme supports the gcompany's goal of creating communities where people can live, work, play and thrive while encouraging individuals to reduce screen time and build stronger social connections.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Unsubsidised fuel prices cut by 10 sen for July 1-8 period
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Finance Ministry announced a 10 sen per litre reduction in unsubsidised RON97, RON95 and diesel prices for the July 1-8 pricing period.
In a statement, the ministry said the adjustment was made in line with the Automatic Pricing Mechanism following a decline in average international fuel prices last week.
RON97 will retail at RM4.00 per litre, while unsubsidised RON95 will be priced at RM3.37 per litre, with unsubsidised diesel reduced to RM3.97 per litre.
The ministry said the prices were announced today to align the new pricing period with the implementation of the Budi Madani Diesel (Budi Diesel) programme from tomorrow.
It said international petroleum prices had continued to moderate from the peak recorded during the Middle East conflict, supported by signs of improving global supply and expectations that negotiations over the conflict could reduce the risk of crude oil supply disruptions.
The ministry added that the global petroleum market had yet to fully stabilise, with risks to crude oil prices, shipping costs and supply security remaining until the conflict is fully resolved and supply chains recover.
While the country's fuel supply remains sufficient, the ministry urged the public to use fuel prudently by planning journeys more efficiently and avoiding unnecessary travel to help conserve national fuel supplies and reduce pressure on subsidy expenditure.
Subsidised fuel prices remain unchanged, with RON95 under the Budi95 programme maintained at RM1.99 per litre, Budi Diesel at RM2.10 per litre, the Subsidised Petrol Control System at RM2.05 per litre, and the Subsidised Diesel Control System at RM2.15 per litre.
The ministry said eligible Malaysians would be able to purchase subsidised diesel at RM2.10 per litre through MyKad verification at petrol stations nationwide under the Budi Diesel programme from July 1.
It said the government would continue adopting a prudent approach to protect consumers from fuel price volatility while ensuring the country's fuel supply remains sufficient and secure.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bursa Malaysia ends on a softer note
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia ended the first half of the 2026 trading year on a softer note, following a range-bound trading session as investors remained cautious amid the lack of fresh domestic catalysts.
Rakuten Trade Sdn Bhd vice-president of equity research Thong Pak Leng said regional equities finished broadly higher, supported by stronger-than-expected business activity data from China and continued strength in technology stocks.
At 5pm, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) eased 1.85 points to 1,664.06 from Monday's close of 1,665.91.
The index opened 1.37 points higher at 1,667.28 and fluctuated between an intraday low of 1,661.80 and a high of 1,669.88 throughout the day.
Market breadth was positive, with gainers outnumbering decliners 562 to 415, while 589 counters were unchanged, 1,124 untraded, and 79 suspended.
Turnover increased to 2.91 billion units valued at RM2.77 billion against 2.71 billion units valued at RM2.13 billion yesterday.
Regionally, Shanghai's SSE Composite Index rose 0.50 per cent to 4,094.40, Japan's Nikkei 225 increased 0.86 per cent to 70,062.32, and South Korea's KOSPI Composite Index gained 0.97 per cent to 8,476.48, while Singapore's Straits Times Index went down 0.73 per cent to 5,170.65.
Thong said on the domestic front, sentiment remained subdued as persistent foreign selling and external uncertainties continued to weigh on investor confidence.
"Nevertheless, the recent market pullback has made valuations of selected blue-chip stocks more attractive, potentially encouraging bargain hunting if global market sentiment remains supportive.
"While near-term volatility is likely to persist, we expect the downside to be cushioned by domestic institutional buying and selective accumulation in fundamentally sound stocks," he told Bernama.
Among the heavyweights, Maybank was two sen lower at RM10.78, and CIMB fell four sen to RM7.41, while Public Bank added one sen to RM4.80, Tenaga Nasional bagged two sen to RM14.28, and IHH was flat at RM8.40.
Of the active counters, Tanco added three sen to 17.5 sen, and Gamuda jumped 17 sen to RM4.37, while Zetrix AI was flat at 76 sen. Dagang NeXchange trimmed half a sen to 41.5 sen, and ACE Market debutant Liftech slipped five sen to 24 sen.
On the top gainers list, Malaysian Pacific Industries garnered 56 sen to RM49.66, Dutch Lady put on 50 sen to RM33.00, United Plantations rose 42 sen to RM33.08, UMS Integration surged 34 sen to RM8.21, and Apollo soared 29 sen to RM6.14.
Among the top losers, Batu Kawan dropped 34 sen to RM21.10, Allianz trimmed 22 sen to RM21.08, Dynafront tumbled 20 sen to 80 sen, AmBank slid 17 sen to RM6.37, and LPI slipped 16 sen to RM14.98.
On the broader market, the FBM Emas Index rose by 5.20 points to 12,385.11, the FBM Top 100 Index added 1.95 points to 12,215.04, and the FBM Emas Shariah Index improved 31.33 points to 12,283.85.
The FBM Mid 70 Index went up 68.75 points to 17,917.46 and the FBM ACE Index garnered 34.14 points to 4,823.43.
Sector-wise, the Financial Services Index slipped 62.36 points to 19,610.66, the Energy Index perked up 4.14 points to 751.64, the Plantation Index gained 29.13 points to 8,854.77, and the Industrial Products and Services Index shed 0.74 of a point to 180.63.
The Main Market volume climbed to 1.52 billion units valued at RM2.48 billion compared with 1.33 billion units valued at RM1.86 billion on Monday.
Warrants turnover expanded to 883.88 million units worth RM111.20 million versus 851.04 million units worth RM110.69 million yesterday.
The ACE Market volume declined to 502.82 million units valued at RM176.25 million from 526.01 million units valued at RM163.67 million previously.
Consumer products and services counters accounted for 163.71 million shares traded on the Main Market, industrial products and services (285.24 million), construction (103.53 million), technology (340.12 million), financial services (77.88 million), property (244.71 million), plantation (29.25 million), real estate investment trusts (16.47 million), closed-end fund (9,000), energy (87.19 million), healthcare (66.28 million), telecommunications and media (48.24 million), transportation and logistics (36.23 million), utilities (24.39 million), and business trusts (63,700).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor polls: 200 Johor PKR members, including vice-chairman, quit for MIC
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI: Two hundred PKR members from its Iskandar Puteri division, including Johor PKR vice chairman M. Murugan, resigned to join MIC on Sunday.
State MIC Youth chief A. Mohan said the former PKR members' resignation and subsequent move signalled stronger grassroots support towards Barisan Nasional.
"Since the beginning of this year, there have been more than 30,000 new applications to join Johor MIC, including these new members, which brought total nationwide new membership to more than 350,000," he said.
Meanwhile, Murugan said he and the other members left PKR due to long-standing dissatisfaction with its current leadership at both state and central levels.
"The decision was made collectively after repeated complaints raised by members were not adequately addressed.
"I have been in PKR for 19 years. I was among the longest-serving members in the Iskandar Puteri branch.
"I have decided to leave PKR and return to MIC after almost 20 years away from the party. I am returning to my original political home in the interests of the Indian community," he said, adding that the group had submitted their membership applications at Dewan Taman Nesa, Skudai.
Murugan said the total number of PKR members leaving the Kota Iskandar branch was around 350, comprising Malays, Chinese and Indians, with the majority from the Indian community.
"Of the 350 who left, about 200 have joined MIC. Others have joined Umno, MCA, Bersama, while some have quit active politics altogether," he said.
Murugan also said he encouraged more PKR members across Johor to join MIC, describing the party as the main platform advocating for the welfare of the Indian community.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: India's Cockroach party seeking education minister's ouster awaits cabinet reshuffle
Article:
NEW DELHI: Leaders of India's youth Cockroach Janta Party neared two weeks of sit-in protests on Tuesday, backed by a well-known activist who has started a hunger strike in support of their demand for the resignation of the education minister.
The protests in the capital Delhi come as sources say the government is weighing significant cabinet changes, with Indian media reporting Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan could be moved from his portfolio.
The CJP, which gained 22 million followers on Instagram within a few days of being set up last month, is demanding his resignation for the leak of question papers for a national medical college entrance examination.
Pradhan, his ministry and the government's chief spokesperson did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
About 100 supporters of the CJP have been gathering daily at the protest site in central Delhi.
"With each passing day, more people are coming here from different parts of India," said 30-year-old party founder Abhijeet Dipke as he and social activist Sonam Wangchuk sat on a makeshift stage beneath a banner calling for Pradhan's removal.
"We are waiting to see what the government decides because there are reports of a cabinet reshuffle. Once that announcement comes, we will decide the next course of action."
Wangchuk is a prominent critic of the government who was arrested last year after violent protests demanding statehood for his native Himalayan federal territory of Ladakh.
Wangchuk said he would undertake a fast that would last six weeks unless he died first.
"But hopefully, we don't have to go that far," he said, lying on a mattress.
"A sensitive government in a democracy listens to the pains of the people, and I hope they will take action."
CJP describes itself as representing "the lazy, the unemployed, and the chronically correct."
Its rapid online rise reflects frustrations among young Indians, who are estimated to make up more than half the country's 1.42 billion population.
India's unemployment rate was 3.1 per cent in 2025 for people aged 15 and above, government data showed, but nearly 10 per cent among those aged 15 to 29, rising to 13.6 per cent in urban areas.
Young people have also been angered by the question paper leaks, which led to the cancellation of the medical college examination taken by 2.3 million aspirants.
It was held for a second time this month after the government deployed military aircraft to transport exam papers and temporarily blocked the Telegram online messaging platform, where it said the leak was spread.
The CJP has drawn criticism from Modi's BJP, with party president Nitin Nabin saying this week that "these virus and cockroach parties can hollow out the country."
"Such people are part of an anti-India gang and only BJP workers can teach them a lesson," he said in a speech.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ministry, TikTok Shop roll out RM180mil cost relief drive
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry and TikTok Shop Malaysia have launched a RM180 million cost-of-living relief initiative.
It features lower seller commission fees, shipping rebates and discounts on essential goods.
Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Armizan Mohd Ali said the collaboration shows how digital commerce platforms can support national efforts to promote local products, ease living costs and empower entrepreneurs.
"I understand that nearly 1,000 entrepreneurs, content creators and members of the e-commerce ecosystem have gathered here today alongside TikTok Shop.
"This gathering enables both parties to strengthen their collaboration and move forward more strategically," he said at the launch during Pesta #JomLokal today.
"Our efforts to address the cost of living are carried out through two approaches simultaneously. First, to stabilise the prices of goods. Second, and equally important, is to increase the people's incomes."
Armizan said the government aims to keep inflation manageable while creating more income opportunities through entrepreneurship and a stronger domestic trade ecosystem.
He said the initiative complements the ministry's recently-launched Jom Malaysia: Jom Beli Produk Malaysia campaign to encourage support for local products while helping Malaysian brands expand into global markets.
"The campaign is no longer just about buying Malaysian goods. We have expanded its scope to include services and encourage all stakeholders to strengthen economic patriotism by supporting local businesses," he said.
Armizan also welcomed TikTok Shop's move to pioneer an online Jualan Ihsan Rahmah, describing it as a timely initiative to help Malaysians cope with rising living costs.
Despite global economic uncertainties and geopolitical tensions, he said TikTok Shop had continued investing to lower business operating costs and improve affordability for consumers.
Armizan said the ministry is on track to table a new law to regulate Malaysia's e-commerce ecosystem before the end of this year.
The proposed legislation will replace the Electronic Commerce Act with a framework aimed at creating a fairer, more transparent, inclusive and progressive digital marketplace.
"We hope this new law will support the growth of Malaysia's e-commerce ecosystem while creating a more progressive and fair environment for platform providers, online and offline businesses, as well as consumers."
TikTok Shop Malaysia strategic partnerships senior director Nur Azre Abdul Aziz said the platform has committed more than RM180 million to support local merchants and consumers.
"This investment will help reduce operating costs for businesses, ease household expenses and generate wider income opportunities across the e-commerce ecosystem.
"The initiative also expands TikTok Shop's zero-commission programme to five additional essential goods sub-categories, bringing the total eligible categories to 56," added Nur Azre.
The new categories, she said, include milk, powdered milk, yoghurt and fermented dairy products.
Commission fees have also been reduced across 39 other essential goods sub-categories by between 0.5 and 9.1 percentage points.
Merchants will also receive an RM2 rebate for every order to help offset nationwide shipping costs.
Consumers can enjoy discounts of up to 30 per cent on groceries and daily necessities, with additional savings available through TikTok Pay.
TikTok Shop has also become the first e-commerce platform to host an online Jualan Ihsan Rahmah, featuring participating #JomLokal grocers such as Hero Market and Kedai FGV.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mara awaits full police report before acting on Johor MRSM bullying allegations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara) is awaiting the full police investigation report before deciding on further action against six junior science college (MRSM) students accused of bullying a student in Johor.
Mara chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said the school's disciplinary committee had yet to make a final decision, pending the report from the authorities.
"An identification exercise was conducted yesterday involving the victim and those suspected to be involved.
"We are still waiting for the full report from the authorities," he told reporters after the Mara Educators Awards Day ceremony here today.
Asyraf said Mara would not compromise on discipline, morality and character-building across its education system.
He said Mara students must not only possess skills, expertise and competency in their respective fields, but also strong morals, identity and values.
"That is the future generation of leaders we want to produce. Therefore, we cannot compromise with bullying, whether physical, moral or mental," he said.
Asyraf said Mara would begin placing more former military personnel as full-time wardens at MRSMs from tomorrow to strengthen discipline and student supervision.
He said the first phase would involve 10 MRSMs this year, with each school to have four full-time wardens, comprising two men and two women.
He said all 58 MRSMs would have full-time wardens from among former military personnel from January.
The move, he added, was aimed at reducing the burden on teachers, who also had teaching duties.
Yesterday, it was reported that six students from an MRSM in Johor were
facing expulsion
after allegedly bullying a junior student.
Asyraf had earlier instructed Mara's education division to convene the college disciplinary committee immediately, giving it 24 hours to investigate the incident.
The six students, who were remanded for two days, will be expelled if found guilty after the disciplinary process.
On the Mara Educators Awards Day, Asyraf said the ceremony was held annually to recognise the contributions and sacrifices of educators from 346 Mara education institutions, spanning kindergartens, MRSMs, TVET Mara, GiatMara and universities.
He said Mara's education system, particularly its TVET stream, had recorded a graduate employability rate of 99.1 per cent, with many graduates earning above minimum wage.
He added that industries from Malaysia and abroad were increasingly seeking strategic partnerships with Mara due to its flexible curriculum.
This included Samsung, which he said was looking to recruit 700 Mara students at salaries of RM3,500 and above.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Myanmar denies Asean request for Suu Kyi meeting
Article:
NAYPYIDAW: Myanmar's authorities have denied a request for Asean's special envoy to meet its deposed and detained democratic leader Aung San Suu Kyi, an administration spokeswoman said Tuesday.
The military plunged Myanmar into civil war in 2021 when it staged a coup ousting the Nobel Peace Prize laureate and ending a decade-long experiment with democracy.
After five years of military rule, coup leader Min Aung Hlaing this year surrendered his post as armed forces chief to take over as president after tightly restricted elections excluding Suu Kyi's party.
In late April he announced Suu Kyi, now 81, would be moved to house arrest – although analysts dismissed the apparent act of mercy as lip service intended to rebrand his unrelenting rule.
The Asean bloc has frozen Myanmar out since the coup but chair nation the Philippines welcomed Suu Kyi's transfer, requesting "brief access" for its Myanmar special envoy.
"Aung San Suu Kyi has been prosecuted under the law and is serving sentences," Myanmar presidential office spokeswoman Khaing Khaing Soe told reporters in Naypyidaw.
"Therefore she is not allowed to meet with international representatives."
Suu Kyi has vanished from the public eye and is serving a lengthy but unspecified sentence on a host of charges rights groups dismiss as fabricated.
"Only after her sentence, she might get permission," Khaing Khaing Soe said, at the presidency's first press conference since the elections.
The Philippines foreign ministry did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Asean has blocked Myanmar from its high-level summits since the coup, but made little progress with its five-point peace plan to end the war through national dialogue with all sides.
Consensus among nations in the bloc is now fraying, analysts say, between those open to seizing on small concessions to bring Myanmar back into the fold and those holding fast on their hardline stance.
Asean foreign ministers are due to meet in Manila late next month.
"If they invite us, we will attend," said presidential office spokeswoman Khaing Khaing Soe.
Min Aung Hlaing presented elections ending in January as a reboot of democracy and a chance for reconciliation in the civil war.
However the polls sidelined Suu Kyi and her hugely popular party, punished dissent with prison time, were blocked by rebels in areas they control and resulted in a walkover win for pro-military parties.
Despite protests from democracy watchdogs over the integrity of the election, Min Aung Hlaing has already enjoyed warm welcomes on presidential trips this month to India and China.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Court dismisses appeal against Penang reclamation project on procedural grounds
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Court of Appeal today dismissed an appeal by seven fishermen and two environmental groups against the Penang South Reclamation (PSR) project, ruling it was filed too late.
A three-member bench comprising Federal Court judge Datuk Azimah Omar and Court of Appeal judges Datuk Wong Kian Kheong and Datuk Ismail Brahim unanimously dismissed the appeal.
Delivering the court's decision, Wong said the appellants should have filed their judicial review application by Dec 1, 2023, but only did so on Dec 29, 2023.
The court, however, also held that the director of the Penang Planning Department (PlanMalaysia) and/or the Penang Planning Committee had no power to grant the planning permission for the reclamation project.
In a statement issued after the ruling, Sahabat Alam Malaysia (SAM) said the finding meant the planning permission for the PSR project was granted without lawful authority, arguing that the Penang government was proceeding with the construction of the island without valid planning approval.
Despite that finding, SAM said the Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal on procedural grounds, effectively leaving the project to proceed.
"SAM is deeply disappointed by today's decision of the Court of Appeal to dismiss the appeal brought by the fishermen and environmental groups against the PSR project despite the court stating that the planning permission granted was without lawful authority.
"The Court of Appeal decided today that the state director of (PlanMalaysia) and/or the Penang Planning Committee had no power to grant the planning permission for the reclamation.
"The logical consequence of this would be that the planning permission for the PSR project would be rendered invalid, and therefore the Penang government is currently constructing an island without valid planning approvals," SAM honorary secretary S. Mageswari Sangaralingam said.
Mageswari said despite acknowledging that the planning authorities had no power to grant planning permission, the court ultimately dismissed the appeal on procedural grounds, effectively sidestepping these substantive concerns.
The appeal was brought by fishermen Zakaria Ismail, Ibrahim Che Rose, Azhar Najamudin, Ismail Ahmad, Shahril Rosli, Omar Hassan and Ramdzan Ishak, together with environmental groups SAM and Jaringan Ekologi dan Iklim (Jedi).
The appellants challenged the planning permission granted on Aug 21, 2023, by the director of the Penang state Planning Department to the Penang government for the project, also known as Silicon Island.
They argued that the project would cause irreversible environmental damage and adversely affect the livelihoods of the local fishing community.
The appellants also alleged that the approval process breached provisions under the Town and Country Planning Act 1976 and the Environmental Quality Act 1974, including failures to comply with planning laws, errors in project referrals, the absence of fresh environmental assessments, and inadequate consideration of the project's environmental and socio-economic impacts.
They sought, among others, an order to invalidate the planning permission, quash the approval, restore the affected area and award compensation for loss of income.
The respondents were the director of the Penang PlanMalaysia director, the Penang Planning Committee, the Penang government, SRS Consortium Sdn Bhd and Silicon Island Development Sdn Bhd.
The state government argued that the judicial review application was time-barred as the appellants were aware of the planning permission by Sept 1, 2023, and should have commenced proceedings by Dec 1 that year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 60,000 passenger trips on first day of LRT3
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The newly opened Shah Alam LRT3 line recorded 60,000 passenger trips on its first day of operations yesterday.
Rapid Rail Sdn Bhd said that this includes overall usage of the Shah Alam Line network, including train services, feeder buses and Rapid KL On-Demand (ROD).
"Of the total, 58,865 journeys were recorded on train services, while another 3,319 journeys were made through Rapid KL's first-last mile network, comprising 1,883 journeys on feeder buses and 1,436 via the ROD service," it said in a statement today.
Rapid Rail said the response reflected strong demand for the new mobility option.
"It not only reflects public confidence in the Shah Alam line, but also shows the need for a rail network linking residential areas, employment centres, educational institutions and commercial hubs in Petaling Jaya, Shah Alam and Klang," it added.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim launched the line, which runs from Johan Setia to Bandar Utama, on June 28 and announced free rides for one month.
Free travel on the Shah Alam lin
e and feeder bus services along the alignment will continue until July 31.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Israel-Lebanon deal likely to prolong stalemate, not end war
Article:
A security deal between Israel and Lebanon risks entrenching a stalemate rather than resolving Israel's underlying conflict with Hizbollah by tying Israel's pullout from southern Lebanon to the Iran-aligned group's disarmament, a condition regional analysts and politicians say is unattainable.
At its core is a bargain few see as workable: Hizbollah has flatly rejected disarmament, and no Lebanese government has the power to enforce it.
With Hizbollah unlikely to disarm, analysts say Israel has political cover to keep an open-ended military presence in southern Lebanon, which it invaded after Hizbollah fired at Israel on March 2 in solidarity with Teheran over the war in Iran.
The deal leaves the Lebanese state trapped between obligations it cannot meet and sovereignty it cannot fully reclaim.
The framework deal also collides with Lebanon's political realities, asking a fragile sectarian state to confront the most powerful armed faction in the country despite a post-civil war system built on power-sharing rather than coercion.
"This is not an agreement, it is an imposed settlement," said a senior Lebanese politician.
The Lebanese army, he said, was neither structured nor equipped to disarm Hizbollah, and expecting it to do so ignored both the group's entrenched military capacity and the fragile sectarian balance on which Lebanon's stability rests.
Analysts say the imbalance is built into the agreement's design, with sweeping obligations placed on Lebanon but no reciprocal guarantee of Israeli withdrawal.
"This agreement has put all the burden on Lebanon," said Michael Young, a Beirut-based analyst, adding that it "creates a structure that allows the Israelis to remain (in southern Lebanon) indefinitely".
Fawaz Gerges, a Lebanese scholar at the London School of Economics and Political Science, said the deal was "born dead" and is structurally flawed, hinging on a condition that is impossible to meet in practice.
Gerges said Israel had already consolidated a buffer zone in southern Lebanon about 8km to 10km deep while tying any future withdrawal to Hizbollah's disarmament.
The terms of the deal risk giving it diplomatic legitimacy, he said, describing it as a political "gift" to Israel.
The conflict in Lebanon has been a central part of diplomacy towards ending the wider United States-Iran war.
Gerges said Washington's deliberate decoupling of the conflicts gave Israel greater freedom of action in Lebanon.
[[nid:1476949]]
The framework agreement signed in Washington affirms that Israel has no claim to Lebanese territory and makes Lebanese army authority in the south contingent on the verified disarmament of non-state armed groups, including Hizbollah.
Israeli President Benjamin Netanyahu portrays the deal as a historic achievement that could lead to broader peace, while Israeli troops remain deployed in a so-called security zone designed to protect its north from potential attack.
About 4,000 people have been killed in Lebanon and a million displaced during Israel's military campaign against Hizbollah.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun welcomed the agreement as a first step towards restoring Lebanon's sovereignty, saying it should allow Lebanese people to return to fully liberated land.
Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri said it amounted to an "agreement of dictates, not one that preserves Lebanon's rights" and said it would not be implemented.
Hizbollah chief Naim Qassem declared the deal "null and void" and a "surrender" and said his group would keep fighting until Israel is forced to leave.
Hizbollah lawmaker Hassan Fadlallah warned of "internal conflict" in Lebanon. Any attempt to forcibly disarm Hizbollah would risk deepening sectarian tensions.
Young said the deal "won't lead us anywhere except to civil conflict, and maybe an insurrection by the Shia community."
Danny Citrinowicz, a regional analyst and former Israeli military intelligence officer, said Hizbollah's dismantlement was "something that would never happen".
"Israel won't withdraw, and Hizbollah won't dismantle," he said.
Citrinowicz said no Israeli prime minister has the domestic political space to withdraw while Hizbollah is still armed and northern Israeli communities remain displaced.
A narrower pact focused on Hizbollah's pullout from south of the Litani River, an expanded Lebanese army deployment and an extension of state authority, would have stood a better chance of success, he said.
Pro-Hizbollah analyst Mohammed Obeid also said the deal was unlikely to be implemented, adding that its provisions were "like explosives", capable of detonating Lebanon's internal stability, as they hinge on state action to disarm Hizbollah.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chin Hin Group announces leadership transition to drive next growth phase
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Chin Hin Group Bhd has announced a strategic leadership transition across its listed companies to reinforce succession continuity and support the next phase of institutional growth.
The company said that the transition coincided with the group's debut in the 2026 Fortune Southeast Asia 500, where it ranked 292nd, reflecting its expanding operational footprint and regional presence.
Under the succession plan, Datuk Seri Chiau Beng Teik will transition from his role as non-executive chairman of the group to become the founder and chairman emeritus of the group.
"Concurrently, he will serve as chairman emeritus for Chin Hin Group Property Bhd, Signature International Bhd, Fiamma Holdings Bhd, and Ajiya Bhd. In this apex advisory capacity, Chiau will continue to provide long-term counsel and strategic mentorship, ensuring the group remains anchored to its foundational disciplines," it said in a statement.
Meanwhile, Datuk Wira Chiau Haw Choon will be appointed as executive chairman of Chin Hin Group, Chin Hin Group Property, Ajiya, Signature International, and Fiamma Holdings.
"Over more than two decades with the group, he has played a central role in transforming Chin Hin from a family-owned enterprise into a professionally managed and diversified integrated group. He has led successive phases of expansion, acquisition, integration and operational transformation while strengthening the group's IntraBuild ecosystem," it said.
To further strengthen leadership continuity, Chin Hin Group executive director Shelly Chiau Yee Wern will expand her responsibilities to include Ajiya and Fiamma Holdings, in addition to her existing roles at Chin Hin Group, Chin Hin Group Property and Signature International.
The transition is underpinned by a robust financial foundation and a market presence spanning 12 countries. As at Dec 31, 2025, Chin Hin Group maintained a net gearing ratio of 0.53 times. Forward earnings visibility remains highly resilient, supported by a RM2.10 billion construction order book, RM2.18 billion in unbilled property sales, and about RM1.28 billion Home and Living order backlog.
"The board views this as a carefully planned step in the group's long-term succession journey, one that reflects the maturity of Chin Hin Group's leadership structure and its continued institutionalisation as it enters the next phase of growth. This is not a change in direction, but the continuation of a long-term plan.
"As Chin Hin Group moves into its next chapter, it remains focused on delivery quality, margin improvement, prudent capital management, stronger governance, digital transformation and sustainability, anchored by its core strengths and a shared culture of One Vision, One System, One Culture," the company added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Housewife jailed 17 years for causing death of 7-month-old adopted daughter
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A housewife broke down in tears after the Sessions Court here today sentenced her to 17 years' imprisonment for causing the death of her adopted 7-month-old daughter through repeated abuse.
Judge Siti Shakirah Mohtarudin handed down the sentence on Siti Subrina Imran, 39, after she pleaded guilty to the charge.
The court ordered the prison term to take effect from her arrest date on July 31, 2025.
Siti Subrina was charged with causing the death of the infant at a condominium unit in Jalan Sri Permaisuri, Cheras, between 10pm on July 29, 2025, and 7am on July 30, 2025.
The charge was framed under Section 304(a) of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum prison sentence of 30 years and a fine upon conviction.
According to the facts of the case, police received a report from the accused's husband stating that their adopted daughter had become unconscious.
Upon arriving at the residence, officers found the victim lying motionless on her back in a baby cot.
Examinations revealed bruising across the victim's face and body. Investigations found that the accused had physically abused the infant by pinching, slapping, and biting her.
The case facts further stated that she had smoked methamphetamine and blown the smoke into the baby's mouth.
Police also recovered WhatsApp messages sent by the accused to her husband, including one stating: "I bruised that child half dead."
Deputy public prosecutor Wafa Zainal Abidin requested a severe and proportionate sentence, considering that the offence claimed the life of a defenceless 7-month-old baby.
"At that age, the victim relied completely on adults for protection, love and care. However, the person entrusted to safeguard the child became the cause of her suffering and death.
"The repeated acts of physical abuse demonstrate intentional conduct. The sentence should send a clear message to society that child abuse resulting in death will not be treated lightly," she said.
During mitigation, the unrepresented Siti Subrina told the court that she has a child with disabilities who suffers from heart disease.
"My husband has also left me," she said tearfully. — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Higher electricity prices push Malaysia's headline inflation to 2.0pct in May: Bank Negara
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Higher electricity prices, resulting from a surcharge due to increased generation costs, contributed to Malaysia's headline inflation edging up to 2.0 per cent in May, said Bank Negara Malaysia.
In its Monthly Highlights for May 2026 released today, the central bank said headline inflation rose to 2.0 per cent in May from 1.9 per cent in April, while core inflation remained stable at 2.0 per cent.
Bank Negara said the slight increase in headline inflation largely reflected price developments of non-core items, particularly vegetables and electricity.
"The increase in electricity prices reflected the imposition of a surcharge following higher generation costs," it said, adding that the pressures were partly offset by lower inflation for domestic air travel and retail fuel, particularly RON97 and diesel.
Meanwhile, it said credit to the private non-financial sector grew by 6.4 per cent in May from 5.8 per cent in April, driven mainly by businesses as reflected in higher growth in outstanding corporate bonds and outstanding business loans.
Bank Negara said outstanding corporate bonds grew by 8.0 per cent from 6.2 per cent in April, while outstanding business loans increased by 7.0 per cent from 6.2 per cent previously.
It said business loan growth increased primarily for working capital purposes, while growth for investment-related loans remained broadly steady.
"Household loan growth was sustained at 5.5 per cent, amid steady loan growth across most purposes," it said.
As for the real sector, Bank Negara said the Manufacturing Industrial Production Index registered higher growth of 8.3 per cent in April, compared with 5.5 per cent in March.
It said export-oriented clusters expanded by 8.5 per cent from 6.7 per cent previously, driven by higher production of electrical and electronics and primary-related products such as refined petroleum and chemical products.
It said growth in domestic-oriented clusters also strengthened to 8.0 per cent from 2.8 per cent in March, reflecting higher production of motor vehicles, food processing products and construction-related materials.
Bank Negara said banks' asset quality remained intact, with gross and net impaired loan ratios broadly unchanged at 1.4 per cent and 1.0 per cent, respectively, underpinned by stable impairments and sustained loan growth.
The banking system also maintained adequate liquidity buffers to withstand potential liquidity shocks, with an aggregate Liquidity Coverage Ratio of 149.2 per cent, it said.
On financial markets, Bank Negara said global market sentiment continued to be influenced by uncertainties surrounding the West Asia conflict and expectations over the US Federal Reserve's policy rate path, although sentiment improved somewhat amid optimism over a potential resolution to the conflict during the month.
"Amid these global developments, the ringgit appreciated slightly by 0.1 per cent against the US dollar, broadly outperforming other regional currencies," it said.
The benchmark 10-year Malaysian Government Securities yield remained broadly stable, while the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI declined 2.3 per cent amid non-resident outflows, it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nearly one million people worldwide became millionaires in 2025, UBS report finds
Article:
ZURICH: Personal wealth in 2025 grew at its fastest pace in years, creating nearly one million new US dollar millionaires worldwide, Swiss bank UBS said in its annual Global Wealth Report published on Tuesday.
Here are some details:
Total personal wealth globally rose by 10.8 per cent last year, up from 4.6 per cent in 2024 and 4.2 per cent in 2023, as strong financial markets boosted growth, UBS found.
There were "more millionaires than ever, everywhere" in 2025, the bank said. The United States, where over 440,000 people became new US dollar millionaires, accounted for almost half of this growth.
Wealth in US dollar terms grew disproportionately quickly in Europe, largely due to last year's depreciation of the dollar compared to the euro, UBS found.
While average wealth rose, inequality has deepened since 2020, UBS said. Median wealth, which better reflects the middle of the scale, declined in most countries, highlighting a growing divide between the wealthiest and the broader population, the bank added.
For the report, UBS analysed 56 markets it estimates to represent over 92 per cent of the world's wealth.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia reports 5th mainland case of H5N1 bird flu
Article:
CANBERRA: Australian authorities on Tuesday confirmed the fifth mainland case of the deadly H5N1 strain of avian influenza, but said there is no evidence of local transmission, Xinhua reported.
Minister for Agriculture, Fisheries, and Forestry, Julie Collins, and Chief Veterinary Officer, Beth Cookson, told reporters in Canberra that testing has detected the H5N1 strain in a migratory giant petrel that was found at a beach on the south coast of Western Australia (WA).
It takes the total number of confirmed cases of the deadly strain in mainland Australia to five, four of which have been detected in WA.
The Australian mainland was the only continent without a case of the highly pathogenic strain, which has caused the deaths of millions of birds and other animals globally since 2020, until it was detected in a migratory bird that was found in southern WA on June 14.
"The detections so far have been in wild migratory seabirds, and there's no evidence of spread to resident populations," Cookson said on Tuesday.
A notice posted on the Department of Agriculture, Fisheries, and Forestry's website said that there is no evidence of mass mortality or of infection in the poultry industry.
The department said that the risk to human health remains low and urged anyone who notices multiple sick or dead birds not to touch them and to report the incident to authorities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Syed Saddiq's lawyer calls Federal Court postponement 'unprecedented' as ruling delayed
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Syed Saddiq Syed Abdul Rahman's lawyer, Datuk Hisyam Teh Poh Teik, described today's Federal Court postponement as "unprecedented," saying it was the first time in his legal career that he had experienced such a situation.
"It is the first time in my practice and the first time in my experience," he said, adding that the decision could not be delivered as one member of the quorum was unwell this morning.
Meanwhile, Muar MP Syed Saddiq said he was disappointed that the long-running case had still not reached finality after six years of proceedings.
"Obviously, I was hoping there would be finality today, especially after six gruelling, to some extent, gruesome years of pursuing justice," he said after brief proceedings at the Federal Court.
He said he respected the court's decision to postpone the ruling, noting that the delay was due to unforeseen circumstances.
"I understand this matter is truly unexpected… when it involves health issues, it is beyond my control. We have to respect that," he said.
The Federal Court has fixed July 13 for its decision.
Court of Appeal President Datuk Seri Abu Bakar Jais, who chaired the three-member bench, said the postponement was due to the absence of judge Datuk Che Mohd Ruzima Ghazali, who is on medical leave.
Other members of the bench were Datuk Collin Lawrence Sequerah and Datuk Che Mohd Ruzima Ghazali.
In November 2023, the High Court convicted the former Bersatu Youth chief and sentenced him to seven years' imprisonment, two strokes of the cane and a RM10 million fine.
In June last year, however, the Court of Appeal overturned the conviction and acquitted him, ruling that the conviction was unsafe.
The prosecution is now seeking to reinstate the High Court decision, a move that could have significant consequences for his political future.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Myanmar jade mine landslide kills five, 15 missing
Article:
YANGON: Monsoon rains triggered the collapse of slag heap at a disused Myanmar jade mine, killing five people scavenging for gem scraps and leaving around 15 more buried missing, state media said Tuesday.
Myanmar has a huge unregulated mining sector and northern Kachin state is the world's largest source of jadeite – considered auspicious in many Asian cultures and hugely lucrative in neighbouring China.
Factions fighting in Myanmar's post-coup civil war control many mines, using profits to fill their coffers while impoverished locals toil underground or scrounge for leftovers to make a living.
"Around 20 freelance jade scavengers searching the site under floodlights were buried when the heap suddenly gave way" late on Sunday in Kachin's Hpakant township, state media said.
"The collapse occurred after days of heavy rain left old mine waste heaps unstable," said newspaper The Global New Light of Myanmar.
"At least five people have been confirmed dead, and around 15 remain missing," it added. "Search teams continue digging for those still missing."
Mass casualty accidents are common at Myanmar's mines – particularly in monsoon season, when rains destabilise sites where workers have dug unsafe shafts or piled up precarious waste heaps.
Those who sift through the discarded rubble for gem fragments missed by the main mining operations are on the bottom rung of the highly profitable industry.
Hpakant has also witnessed vicious combat in recent months between the Myanmar military and opposition forces, vying for control of jade mining operations.
Myanmar has been consumed by a civil war since the military toppled the elected government of Aung San Suu Kyi in 2021, ending a decade-long experiment with democracy.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NUJ joins calls to refer Cybercrimes Bill to select committee, cites need to protect journalists' sources
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The National Union of Journalists Malaysia (NUJM) has called for the Cybercrimes Bill 2026 to be referred to a parliamentary special select committee (PSSC), saying further scrutiny is needed to ensure the law does not undermine journalists' ability to protect confidential sources.
NUJM representative Teh Athira Mohd Yusof said stakeholders affected by the bill must be consulted to provide insights and clarify concerns before the legislation is passed.
"The rights of journalists must be upheld.
"If not, how can they source information and protect their sources?" she said.
The union echoed concerns raised yesterday by the
Malaysian Media Council
, which urged the government to refer the bill to a PSSC for further study and wider consultation.
The bill grants extensive surveillance and interception powers to prevent any crime under any law, which are not limited to the cybercrimes it attempts to address.
Earlier today, three non-governmental organisations (NGOs) — Article 19, the Centre for Independent Journalism (CIJ) and Sinar Project —  also urged the government to halt the second reading of the Cybercrimes Bill 2026 and refer it to a parliamentary special select committee, expressing concerns that the legislation fails to meet minimum standards set out in the United Nations Cybercrime Convention.
NGOs Article 19, the Centre for Independent Journalism (CIJ) and Sinar Project expressed concerns that the
cybercrime legislation failed
to meet minimum standards set out in the United Nations Cybercrime Convention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fuziah wishes ex-PKR members well after mass exit, says she hopes they get senior posts
Article:
JOHOR BARU: PKR secretary-general Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh has brushed off the departure of former Johor PKR state leadership council vice-chairman M. Murugan and his supporters, downplaying their decision as being driven by frustration over positions.
Commenting on Murugan's announcement that he and around 200 supporters left the party to join the Iskandar Puteri MIC division, Fuziah said she found the reasoning behind their exit strange.
"I read his statement, and among the reasons for leaving is that they were disappointed at not getting positions.
"So, I wish them all the best. Hopefully, they will get big positions over there," she said.
Two days ago, Murugan, popularly known as 'Cikgu Rony', reportedly said the decision was made out of frustration with both the state and central PKR leadership, claiming they had been indifferent toward long-standing complaints.
Murugan also claimed that a total of 350 members from the Kota Iskandar PKR branch had quit the party.
He added that some of them have joined Umno, MCA, and Parti Bersama Malaysia (Bersama), while others have decided to withdraw from active politics entirely.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested in Kota Kinabalu hotel over Lawas parang attack
Article:
LAWAS: A 38-year-old man who went into hiding at a hotel in Kota Kinabalu after seriously injuring another man in a parang attack was arrested early on Friday.
Lawas police chief DSP Liong Tindan said the suspect was arrested at 2am by a team from the district Criminal Investigation Department and their Sabah contingent counterparts.
The suspect is wanted to assist investigations into the incident at Kampung Sinampuan, Sundar, on June 7.
"Police tracked down the suspect before raiding the hotel room and arresting him," Liong said.
"A parang used in the incident was also seized."
Liong said the suspect tested positive for methamphetamine and amphetamine.
He added that checks showed the suspect has five previous criminal and drug-related offences.
"The motive for the incident is still under investigation. The case is being investigated under Section 324 of the Penal Code and Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952," he said.
In a separate case, a 45-year-old man was arrested after he allegedly ran amok and threatened his mother and aunt with a parang at a house in Ba'kelalan last Thursday.
In the 2.52pm incident, the 66-year-old woman and her younger sister managed to flee to a relative's house before lodging a police report.
Police then rushed to the scene, arrested the suspect at the house and seized a parang.
The suspect has two previous criminal cases. The case is being investigated under Section 506 of the Penal Code for criminal intimidation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thai Airways crew member arrested in Australia over heroin haul
Article:
SYDNEY: A Thai airline employee was charged with importing more than a kilogram (2.2 pounds) of heroin into Australia after customs officials found the drug concealed in tote bags, Australian Federal Police said.
The 26-year-old woman, who national flag carrier Thai Airways said was working as a flight attendant when she arrived at Melbourne Airport on June 25, had 12 tote bags screened by border officers, police said.
The heroin, found hidden in the lining of the bags, had an estimated street value of A$500,000 (US$343,300).
She faces one count of importing and one count of possession of a marketable quantity of a border controlled drug. Each charge carries a maximum penalty of 25 years' imprisonment.
The woman was remanded in custody on June 26 and is due to appear before Melbourne Magistrates' Court on Sep 14.
Thai Airways said in a statement it had strict rules governing the conduct of all employees and "stands ready to fully cooperate with the relevant authorities throughout the process."
"The AFP remains unwavering in its efforts to target individuals who use their employment or community standing to support drug trafficking," said Australian Federal Police Acting Commander Simone Butcher.
Australian Border Force Commander Clint Sims said criminal syndicates continued to target trusted insiders, including airline crew, to smuggle illicit substances into Australia. - REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: South Korea, Ukraine hold 'constructive' talks about North Korean POWs
Article:
SEOUL: South Korea and Ukraine held "constructive" talks Tuesday about the fate of two North Korean soldiers captured while fighting in Russia, Seoul said Tuesday.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun and Ukrainian counterpart Andriy Sybiga discussed the matter during talks in Seoul.
The soldiers were captured by Ukrainian forces in January last year while fighting for Russia in the Kursk region.
North Korea and Russia have demanded the repatriation of the pair, but rights groups and experts have warned they could face severe punishment if returned to Pyongyang.
The soldiers have asked to be sent to South Korea, which would in effect amount to defection.
Seoul is seeking their transfer on the grounds that they are citizens of a single Republic of Korea envisaged in its constitution.
North and South Korea remain technically at war after their 1950-53 conflict ended in an armistice, not a peace treaty.
The nations are separated by a demilitarised zone, but South Korea considers the entire Korean peninsula as one country.
The ministers "engaged in constructive discussions" on the soldiers' future on Tuesday, a South Korean foreign ministry spokesman told reporters.
They agreed to be guided by "international law and humanitarian principles."
"The government will continue to make diplomatic efforts to advance and resolve the issue of the North Korean prisoners of war," the spokesman said.
Writing on X, Sybiga said he and Cho discussed the matter "in detail."
It has been reported that North Korean authorities instructed troops to commit suicide rather than allow themselves to be captured while fighting for Russia.
One of the two held by Ukraine told South Korean broadcaster MBC earlier this year: "If I am not brought to South Korea, I will end up dying."
North Korean leader Kim Jong Un praised North Korean troops killed in Ukraine in April for taking part in "death-defying hand-to-hand fights and heroic suicidal explosions... without hesitation", according to the Korean Central News Agency.
Kyiv had assured Seoul the two soldiers would not be repatriated to North Korea against their will, Cho said in March.
Analysts say North Korea has received financial assistance, military technology, food and energy from Russia in exchange for missiles, munitions and thousands of troops for Moscow's war in Ukraine.
The two countries had signed a mutual defence treaty in 2024, obliging each to provide military assistance "without delay" if either comes under attack.
More than 7,000 North Korean troops were killed or wounded while fighting alongside Russian forces in the Kursk region during the 2024-2025 campaign, the Kyiv Independent quoted Ukraine's military intelligence agency as saying this week.
Sybiga said he and Cho also discussed "shared challenges stemming from the deepening cooperation between Moscow and Pyongyang."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor Intelligent Parking system hit by outage
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Selangor Intelligent Parking (SIP) system has been hit by a temporary outage, affecting several services, particularly parking payment functions.
The disruption has affected users in Selangor as well as several areas outside the state that use the same platform.
Rantaian Mesra Sdn Bhd (RMSB), a company owned by Menteri Besar Selangor (Incorporation), said the SIP platform had been temporarily suspended to facilitate recovery efforts and safeguard system and user data integrity.
In a statement, RMSB said technical inspections were underway to ensure the system was stable and met safety, data integrity and reliability standards before services resumed.
"During this disruption, no parking charges or compounds will be imposed on users," the statement said.
RMSB said every effort was being made to restore services as soon as possible.
It advised the public to obtain updates only through RMSB's and SIP's official communication channels.
"Those requiring assistance or further information can contact the SIP Customer Service Support Line at 03-5650 0913 or email sip@rmsb.my," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perak-born man loses citizenship appeal despite having Malaysian father and passport
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, June 30 — Perak-born Haritharan Mugunthan, 25, today failed again in court to be declared a Malaysian citizen.
This is despite him being born to a Malaysian father, and having once been issued a Malaysian passport when he was just more than one year old.
The Court of Appeal unanimously decided today that Haritharan had failed to fulfill the Federal Constitution's requirements to be a Malaysian automatically.
Court of Appeal judge Datuk Faizah Jamaludin noted that Haritharan referred to factors such as his past Malaysian passport issued in 2002, and his MyKid number or the identification number given out to Malaysian children.
“We are mindful of the appellant's personal circumstances, however this appeal cannot be decided on sympathy.
“The question is whether the appellant has established on all evidence before this court that he is a Malaysian citizen by operation of law under the Federal Constitution,” she said when reading excerpts of the court's judgment via Zoom today, referring to Haritharan as the appellant.
Court says Federal Constitution decides whether someone is a Malaysian, not their passport
At one point, Faizah noted Haritharan had in this citizenship case relied on his birth certificate, the 2022 Malaysian passport, MyKid number, school records, immunisation records, him living in Malaysia and other administrative dealings.
“We accept that these matters explain why the appellant and his family may have believed that he had been treated as Malaysian.
“However, those documents are not the source of citizenship. Citizenship by operation of law arises only if the requirements of the Federal Constitution are satisfied,” she said, ultimately concluding that Haritharan had not met the constitutional requirements for Malaysian citizenship.
Haritharan had previously pointed out other cases where individuals were granted Malaysian citizenship, such as footballers becoming naturalised citizens, and also Pahang-born Azimah Hamzah whose refugee parents became naturalised Malaysians and who at age 38 also succeeded in becoming a naturalised citizen.
Haritharan had also referred to the case of Klang-born Nalvin Dhillon, whose Malaysian father and non-Malaysian mother were unmarried when he was born.
Nalvin also had a Malaysian passport before discovering that the National Registration Department (NRD) recorded him as a non-citizen, and ultimately received his MyKad after winning his citizenship case at the High Court.
But the Court of Appeal said those cases do not decide the constitutional questions in Haritharan's case, and that each citizenship case must be decided on its own evidence and according to the constitutional route to citizenship used in each case.
Why having a Malaysian father did not result in Haritharan being recognised as Malaysian
Although Haritharan was born in Malaysia to a Malaysian father, he cannot be entitled to Malaysian citizenship under the Federal Constitution, the court ruled.
This is because his Malaysian father and Thai mother were not married when he was born, which means his birth is considered in law to be illegitimate.
Based on Section 17 of the Federal Constitution's Second Schedule's Part III, an illegitimate child's parent can only refer to the child's mother, the court said.
Applying Section 17, the Court of Appeal said this means Haritharan cannot rely on his Malaysian father's citizenship, but will have to take on the Thai mother's citizenship.
Since there is no evidence that Haritharan's mother was a Malaysian or a permanent resident when he was born, he has failed to meet the Federal Constitution's condition of having at least one Malaysian or one permanent resident parent at the time of his birth.
Although Haritharan's mother left him when he was aged about one year and two months old, the Court of Appeal said this does not mean he is a “new born child found exposed” or abandoned, and that means he cannot use Section 19B of the Federal Constitution's Second Schedule’s Part III for his citizenship bid.
Court says it's whether you were ‘born’ a citizen of another country, not whether you are another country's citizen now
Haritharan also tried to argue he should be a Malaysian under the Federal Constitution because he was born here and is not born a citizen of any country.
While Haritharan said he does not have citizenship from any country, the court said this would not prove he was “not born” with such other citizenship: “The question is not merely whether he is presently recognised by another state as its citizen. The question is whether, at birth, he was not born a citizen of any country.”
The government had argued that Thai law meant that anyone born to a Thai father or mother would acquire Thai nationality by birth, even when born outside of Thailand.
The court said it is not necessary for it “to make an absolute finding” that Haritharan was born a Thai citizen, but it is enough to say he has not proven he was not born a citizen of any country.
When contacted by
Malay Mail
, Haritharan's lawyer R. Renuga Ramayah confirmed that her client will be appealing against the Court of Appeal's decision.
In this case, senior federal counsel Shahidah Nafisah Leman appeared for the government of Malaysia, the National Registration Department's director-general, and the Home Ministry's secretary-general.
The other judges on the panel today are its chair Datuk Azhahari Kamal Ramli and Datuk Hayatul Akmal Abdul Aziz.
Two sisters born to a Malaysian father also lose citizenship appeal today
In a similar case involving two sisters born in Kuala Lumpur to a Malaysian father and a non-Malaysian mother, the Court of Appeal today also unanimously decided that they failed to meet the constitutional requirements to be Malaysian citizens.
In reading out the excerpts of the judgment, Court of Appeal judge Azhahari also applied the same Section 17 as the parents were not married when the two sisters were born, which means they have to take on their non-Malaysian mother's citizenship instead of the Malaysian father's citizenship.
Since the mother is a Filipino citizen, the judge said the two sisters are not entitled to Malaysian citizenship.
Although the two sisters have certificates from the Philippines' embassy here to say they had never applied for or been issued a passport from the Philippines, Azhahari said “those certificates do not establish they were not Filipino citizens at birth, nor does it establish they did not acquire Filipino citizenship within one year” after birth.
In other words, the judge said the lack of a passport from the Philippines is not the same as proof that they are not citizens of the Philippines, and ultimately dismissed their citizenship bid.
The two sisters (identified only as SL and SF by
Malay Mail
for privacy purposes) were represented today by lawyer Larissa Ann Louis, while lawyer Ranee Sreedharan held a watching brief for non-governmental organisation DHRRA Malaysia.
Senior federal counsel Syahriah Shapiee represented the Malaysian government, the home minister, and the NRD director-general in this case.
Today, the same Court of Appeal panel delivered its decision in two other cases, with a Klang-born girl declared as Malaysian, while a woman failed her citizenship appeal as the court said she was no longer a “new born” when found abandoned around age one at a Pahang bus stop.
Recommended reading:
In court, Ipoh-born man questions why footballers granted citizenship, while he still waits despite having Malaysian father
Stateless his whole life, 38-year-old Perak man wants to be Malaysian; says late mother born pre-Merdeka should have been citizen too
After years of being stateless, High Court recognises Klang-born man with Malaysian-Filipino parents as citizen
Born in Pahang to refugee-turned-Malaysian parents, Azimah finally gets citizenship and IC at age 38
Court of Appeal grants Malaysian citizenship to abandoned Klang teen, rejects Pahang woman's appeal
